# tape-six-fast-check — full reference

> Property-based testing plugin for tape-six powered by fast-check. Registers two tester methods — `t.prop()` (properties) and `t.scheduler()` (race conditions) — each reporting one aggregate counted assertion with structured counterexample/seed/path data read from `fc.check()` run details (never parsed from text). ES modules only; no build step; no runtime dependencies. Peer dependencies: tape-six ^1.16.0 (`registerTesterMethod`, `Tester.reportAssertion`), fast-check ^4 (`RunDetails`).

## Install

npm install --save-dev tape-six-fast-check tape-six fast-check

Both peers are supplied by the consumer; the plugin pins neither.

## Registration model

Importing the package is a side effect that installs both methods on tape-six's `Tester.prototype` via `registerTesterMethod`:

```js
import 'tape-six-fast-check';
```

Import it once in every test file that uses `t.prop` / `t.scheduler`. A name collision with a different implementation throws loudly at import time (tape-six's plugin rule). The module has no runtime exports.

## await t.prop(arbitraries, predicate, options?, msg?)

Runs `fc.check(fc.asyncProperty(...arbitraries, predicate))` and reports **one aggregate assertion** on the current test, regardless of how many cases fast-check executed. Returns the full `RunDetails` object from `fc.check()`.

Parameters:

- `arbitraries` — non-empty **array** of fast-check arbitraries, one per predicate argument. `TypeError` (rejected promise) on an empty or non-array value.
- `predicate` — `(...values) => boolean | void | Promise<boolean | void>`. Sync or async; the plugin wraps it in `fc.asyncProperty`, so both work identically. **Contract: throw or return `false` to fail; any other outcome passes.** Do not call `t.*` assertions inside the predicate — it runs up to `numRuns` times and every inner assertion would re-report on each run. `TypeError` on a non-function.
- `options` — `fc.check()` parameters passed through verbatim: `numRuns` (default 100), `seed`, `path`, `endOnFailure`, `interruptAfterTimeInMs`, `skipEqualValues`, `verbose`, `examples`, … The message may take this slot when no options are needed.
- `msg` — assertion name; defaults to `'property holds'`.

Reporting:

- **Success:** `{ok: true, message, marker, operator: 'prop'}` — a lean counted pass.
- **Failure:** additionally `expected: true` and `actual: {counterexample, seed, path, numRuns, numShrinks, error}` — all read from `RunDetails` (`counterexample`, `seed`, `counterexamplePath`, `numRuns`, `numShrinks`, `errorInstance`). No text parsing anywhere.
- The assertion's source location points at the `t.prop()` call site (marker captured in the method's own frame; tape-six resolves `stackList[1]`).

Example:

```js
import test from 'tape-six';
import fc from 'fast-check';
import 'tape-six-fast-check';

test('JSON round-trips plain objects', async t => {
  await t.prop(
    [fc.dictionary(fc.string(), fc.jsonValue())],
    o => JSON.stringify(JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(o))) === JSON.stringify(o),
    {numRuns: 200},
    'parse(stringify(o)) === o'
  );
});
```

Replay a failure exactly (values from the failed run's structured data):

```js
await t.prop([fc.array(fc.integer())], mySortIsStable, {
  seed: -1651341797,
  path: '25:3:1',
  endOnFailure: true
});
```

Using the returned details:

```js
const details = await t.prop([fc.nat()], n => n >= 0);
// details.failed, details.numRuns, details.seed, details.counterexample, …
```

## await t.scheduler(body, options?, msg?)

Race-condition sugar: runs `fc.check(fc.asyncProperty(fc.scheduler(act?), body))`. Same aggregate reporting (operator `'scheduler'`, default message `'scheduled property holds'`), same replay mechanics, same return value (`RunDetails<[Scheduler]>`).

Parameters:

- `body` — `(s: Scheduler) => boolean | void | Promise<boolean | void>`. Wrap promises with `s.schedule(promise)` or functions with `s.scheduleFunction(fn)`, then drain with `await s.waitAll()` (or `s.waitFor(promise)`); fast-check permutes the resolution order across runs to explore interleavings. Throw or return `false` to fail. `TypeError` on a non-function.
- `options` — `fc.check()` parameters **plus** optional `act`, which is split out and forwarded to `fc.scheduler({act})` (React-style act wrappers). The message may take this slot.
- `msg` — assertion name.

Example — detecting an order-dependent bug:

```js
test('finds the interleaving bug', async t => {
  const details = await t.scheduler(async s => {
    let value = null;
    s.schedule(Promise.resolve()).then(() => (value = 'first'));
    s.schedule(Promise.resolve()).then(() => (value = 'second'));
    await s.waitAll();
    return value === 'second'; // holds only under one of the two interleavings
  });
  // under a fixed {seed} this is deterministic; the failure carries the seed for replay
});
```

## TypeScript

`index.d.ts` augments tape-six's `Tester` interface via `declare module 'tape-six'`, so both methods are typed after the side-effect import. Predicate argument types are inferred from the arbitraries tuple:

```ts
import type {RunDetails, Scheduler} from 'fast-check';

const details: RunDetails<[number, string]> = await t.prop(
  [fc.integer(), fc.string()],
  (n, s) => typeof n === 'number' && s.length >= 0
);
```

Exported helper types:

- `ArbitraryTuple<Ts extends [unknown, ...unknown[]]>` — maps a value tuple to the matching arbitraries tuple.
- `PropPredicate<Ts>` — `(...values: Ts) => boolean | void | Promise<boolean | void>`.
- `SchedulerBody` — `(s: Scheduler) => boolean | void | Promise<boolean | void>`.
- `SchedulerOptions` — `Parameters<[Scheduler]> & {act?: SchedulerAct}`.

## Design constraints (for contributors)

- One aggregate assertion per call; failure data is structured, never parsed from fast-check's report text (the tape-six family "events stay uniform" rule).
- Marker (`new Error()`) is captured in each public method's own frame so the reported location is the user's call site.
- No runtime dependencies; tape-six and fast-check remain peer dependencies.
- v1 predicate contract is throw/return-`false`; a scoped sub-tester materializing only the shrunk failing run is a possible later refinement.

## Browser usage

tape-six's browser testing serves plain ES modules with an import map (`tape6-server`; see the tape-six wiki pages "Set-up tests" and "Environment ‐ Browsers"). Extend that map for this plugin:

- `"tape-six-fast-check": "/node_modules/tape-six-fast-check/index.js"`
- `"fast-check": "/node_modules/fast-check/lib/fast-check.js"`
- the seven `pure-rand` subpaths fast-check imports, each mapped to its `.js` file under `/node_modules/pure-rand/lib/esm/` (`distribution/uniformBigInt`, `distribution/uniformInt`, `generator/congruential32`, `generator/mersenne`, `generator/xoroshiro128plus`, `generator/xorshift128plus`, `utils/skipN`) — import maps don't apply a package's `exports` table, and the subpath list mirrors fast-check 4.9.0.

Alternatively one CDN entry replaces the fast-check and pure-rand lines: `"fast-check": "https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/fast-check@4/+esm"`. The complete map is in README § In the browser.

## Links

- README: https://github.com/uhop/tape-six-fast-check#readme
- tape-six: https://github.com/uhop/tape-six
- fast-check: https://fast-check.dev/
